Oh, last time was probably an event, some sort of conference or trade show.
I don't recall which one, but it probably pulled me down there.
All right.
My wife also studied at Baylor College of Medicine, so that brought me out there many years ago.
Super cool, super cool.
Well, for today, what I had planned was really just to kind of catch you up to speed on the evaluation that I've gone through with Mark, Stephen, Jason, and Kelly.
I'm not sure how much context you have on growthx and what we're doing here, but happy to use the next 25 minutes, however is most helpful to you.

Is that changing?
It's just the two of us?
Yeah, just the two of us today.

Yeah, I think that's important.

So I really want to get to know both of you.
There's a certain amount of element of risk involved in that, but I think what we could do is we can still use the time today and the two of us can connect.
You can get me up to speed.
But, yeah, I think Mark has given me some context, and I'm generally supportive of working with you guys, but I haven't given this all that much time.
And before we kind of proceed, I kind of need to be fully body, if you know what mean.
Makes perfect sense.
Yeah, let me start with like a quick overview and demo, and then we can talk tactically around some questions you have and make sure that Marcel joins the next meeting for sure.
And apologies for that.
You know, I definitely intended to have him here today and got a curveball myself on why he wasn't able to.
But the idea here is that we're an end-to-end partner.
So very different from the typical agency.
We're going to come in and identify the right strategy for AI search, but also SEO.
We're going to create content at a scale that drives results much faster.
So five articles per week is what we focus on.
And we've got the flexibility to think beyond just publishing to your blog, right?
We can do editorial, but we can also do programmatic.
For some of our clients, we've done more custom use cases, though we set up a custom pipeline for Webflow, ingesting all of their docs to create these integrations pages.
We've also set up a pipeline for Lovable, where we'll actually use their API to create apps in this template library, all optimized for search, that users can preview or even remix.
And so we do have flexibility with our model.
We've the Thank

Which is, we have an internal platform, experts in the loop, using our AI workflows, and then forward deployed engineers, AI engineers, design engineers, we can really kind of meet you where you need us, but most companies, it makes sense to start with editorial, right, with something that's going to directly drive visibility in AI search, and allow you to capture higher intent, organic traffic.
So the reason we feel like we're positioned as the best partner in this space is, it all comes from Marcel's experience building organic content engines at every stop in his career.

He's never spent much on the paid side, and he's got a story around a content engine that he's built from places like IBM and HP, all the way through to, you know, being the marketing leader at high growth startups that had successful exits.
And DeepGram is really where he started using AI workflows to figure out, you know, where can
To really inform the software that our clients eventually use.
So everything today is really done as an extension of your team.
And the relationship is that your team would review the artifacts we create, which is the context for our workflows.
And then the drafts of the articles, which are also reviewed by our experts internally before we're staging and publishing them in your CMS.
Any questions at a high level, that's essentially what you're getting from the partnership with us.
I'm sorry, you're on mute right now.
So essentially we're kind of outsourcing content engineering to an agency here.
And you're overseeing, you're responsible for all the tooling and the production of the content, which is everything from blog posts to more programmatic listing optimization.
So you've got...
So you've got...
Tools, listicles, comparison pieces.
And then there is about 40 thought leadership pieces in their learn section.
And for Augment, you know, they've seen their traffic go from around 20K monthly organic to 70K in six months.
And then they've shut off their paid ad spend.
Whereas on the AI search side, the search they care the most about is being, you know, the best AI coding tool for an enterprise.
And we're having them positioned ahead of cursor in that answer.
And then you're starting to see our articles get cited for some of their top competitors.
In addition to seeing their share of voice across all of these prompts that they're tracking grow from, you know, around 0% at the start of the partnership to today, 30%.
And at the end of the year, almost neck and neck.
So this is, you know, the goal, right, is to create content that helps position them in the answer the way they.
And what is the, what's the team that you're working with at Augment Code?
How are they resourcing this on their side?
Because there's as much work in managing an agency or a partner like this as there is in the work that you all do.
So I just want to know how you kind of set, how they've set that up and what's the optimum way to work with you guys?
Yeah, I would say streamlining the review process, right?
We need to publish to get signals and we review it a lot internally.
But if there's a committee of folks reviewing every article we create, each person has a different point of view around each sentence and sometimes even each word.
So I would say, you know, the person that's responsible for organic growth needs to be our direct point of contact.
And then the person that would be responsible for creating the content that would drive organic growth needs to be closely involved.
It could be one of those two folks that's reviewing our content, probably the content person, as opposed to like the head of growth.

But both people should be working with us to make sure we're aligned on, you know, at the beginning of the relationship, we're going to create a roadmap of all these different assignments that we can execute on that are connected to the topic clusters where you want authority.
And so I think both people, right, need to be working with us so that we can agree on these are the right topic clusters to prioritize.
You know, these are the assignments that we should start with based on where we want to see visibility increase first.
And then so let's say typically you have two dedicated point of contacts at your customer accounts and that gives you a little bit of agility and speed or would you have more than that?
So like let's say augment code or ramp, are you working with more than one content reviewer or are you keeping it narrow and tight?
Yeah, we're keeping it narrow and tight in those scenarios where we've really like found the one or two reviewers that need to be involved in the process.
But as we start in our strategy sprint, one thing that we're doing is we're developing artifacts in week one.
And I would say this is a place where a product marketer, someone like Kelly, can be extremely helpful.
Because what we'll be doing is we'll actually be creating the context that personalizes the workflows that help us do the research and create the article drafts.
So just to give you a quick example here, Engine is in the business travel space.
And for Engine, one thing we identified is that they want to have a industry focused go to market approach with their content.
And so what we did in week one was we created these artifacts for each industry that go super deep into everything that the operations manager might be thinking about.
Relative to business travel.
So these fears and uncertainties, these motivations, how you win or lose their trust, pain points, it's all connected back to Engine and the software's use case.
Same thing for the finance manager, same thing for the project manager.
But as you can imagine, right, as we're creating like products and services artifacts, this is where a product marketer can really make sure that we're capturing everything correctly.
Because we want to get the context right before we start to try to scale creating content.
But because we've got the workflows built, what's nice about this is we'll have artifacts in week one ready to review.
We'll have the content strategy and roadmap ready by week three.
And then we'll start creating those first articles by week four.
And in most scenarios, I would say we're creating 10 plus articles by the end of the strategy sprint, as opposed to just the five that you're seeing here.
As soon as we're on the same page with...
The quality of the artifacts and the first article, then we can really start to create more on that five-per-week type of basis.
Excellent.
So there's a human-in-the-loop component to this.
You're offshoring that, I think, in the Philippines.
Did I hear that from somebody?
And how effectively is that kind of time zone coverage working, and how is that managed?
Yeah, we have folks all over the world.
I would say there's like 30 countries that are represented at GrowthX today.
The first person that you'd be working with in the strategy sprint is going to be someone who comes from a background leading organic growth.
So William is doing a lot of our strategy sprints today.
He's been head of content marketing.
He started his own content marketing agency.
Another person that is commonly working with clients, Kathy.
Kathy.
Kathy.
Kathy.

She led growth at more technical organizations, so she's been at GitHub, she worked on the cyber team at HP earlier in her career, and these are the folks that would be the point of contact at the beginning.

And then when you think about the ongoing execution, Ada is a great example of someone who's based in Bosnia, but prior to growthx, she was writing for SEMrush, she was working for Animals, which is, you know, one of the well-known content marketing agencies out there.
And she's often, you know, doing a lot of the editing when it comes to creating the content for an assignment, reviewing the drafts and revising them before they are sent to the client.
And so these are just like some of the folks that work on the delivery org for our clients.
In terms of the entire delivery team are...
Chief Content Officer, Matthew Panzarino, he was the Editor-in-Chief at TechCrunch for almost a decade.
And so the idea here is that, you know, anyone who's working with one of our clients, they come from a background in SEO or in leading growth.
Excellent.
And your full-time staff, how much of this is like, how much of the work is done by your full-time staff versus your kind of freelance or advisor network?
Because I'm basically trying to understand how you're scaling this.
Because what I've found with services, you know, in a long history of working with great agencies is that scaling it too much, just things start to fall apart.
So how are you going to maintain that control as you scale?
Totally fair question.
Most everybody that's on the delivery org is in-house.
If we're working with a freelancer, they are doing edits on articles that have basically been created with the machine fine-tuned by the in-house folks who work on several customer accounts.
We also do two kickoffs a week.
So we intentionally don't say yes to everybody and make sure that we have a really good partnership fit before we commit to adding them to our kickoff calendar.

To your point, our vision is that we're bringing more of this into the client's hand and that the engine is strong enough that we don't have this huge dependency on the human side to be able to create great content.
And so when you think about, you know, a piece of content being created today, to show you an example here, for Lovable, we've also created all these guides for them.
And for this specific article, it's an article about...
I'm out.
The cost of starting a blog for an entrepreneur.
And so each of these steps in the process is a workflow that we've built that's using all of those artifacts as custom inputs.
So in this scenario, there's a topic, there is information around the structure of the content that the managing editor is giving.
There are the artifacts around the personas that this article is being written for.
And then what happens is we use different models and specialized tools for research like Tavli, EXA, and then all of that informs the research that you're seeing here.
And so this research, what's happening in the background is it's generating a research plan of who is this assignment being written for?
What's the objective?
What are the questions we need to answer and the sources that we should use to answer those questions?
It's synthesizing the results.
It's evaluating.
And the quality, and if it doesn't hit a certain quality score, it's running the research agentically.

And then if you look into our workflow catalog here, what you can see is that for that research workflow, we are actually, for each step, leveraging this really detailed prompt to go along with the custom inputs for the client.
And so this is the instructions for generating that research plan.

And because these are workflows that have been stitched together, if we need to customize the workflow for a client, which we've done for companies like Biologica, who had doctors reviewing every article that we were creating, because they were, you know, for a company that's selling supplements to women at different stages in their life, you'll see that along with that research workflow, there's custom steps that we've built, like the product integration, like the compliance review.
And so I guess what I'm saying in short here, Michael, is that the human editing is part of the process for sure.
And it's the expert in the loop is critical to, you know, being able to differentiate.
But it's the workflows that are the real piece that stands out in terms of like just being able to create quality content agnostic of the human reviewing and helping personalize it to your brand.
And so what we allow our team to do is we allow our team to make changes to the article at any step in the process.
And they can also use the context to move faster.
So I could take the personas artifact here and I could say add three bullet points tailored to a entrepreneur that is also a software, a full stack software developer.
And then it would be able.
To use the context that we have, use all the research that's been done to provide very, you know, relevant additional text specific to the command that our expert in the loop might be giving it.
So, you know, I think just kind of as a quick summary here, right, like we've created all the content that you see here for Sentinel One.
We've created a lot of very technical content for Redis across their blog, Airbyte.
We've done a lot of these data engineering resources, and I've got several examples I could show you of clients who, you know, when they started with GrowthX, their organic traffic was in one place.
Fast forward six months later, you're seeing anywhere from two to, in a very unique scenario with Discern, almost 100x increase in the traffic that's being driven.
So, you know, I think, like, even from my own use case, I've got this, like, blog that has no traffic going to it.
And I just used our workflows out of the box.
I didn't make any edits really to them at all.
It's all about, like, underrated music.
And I mean, one thing that blew my mind was, like, you know, Bad Bunny performed at the Super Bowl.
And I'm the first result for the article that I created using GrowthX.
And I have no traffic if you look at my SEMrush.

So, you know, a lot of it is just building the workflows that can do deeper research, that can do stronger cross-linking and source linking, but then making sure that we're audience first by doing the strategy sprint as the first step in our process.

There's no way for us to take a prompt and deconstruct.

actually, maybe that's what I'll do.

of the artifacts for Engine, you can see that the team has updated this artifact 25 times over the year plus that we've been working with that team because this is an artifact that we were able to grab examples that they really liked.
